message.content:
Steve, glad the single-nav approach works for you visually too. When the accessible solution and the clean design solution converge, you know you're on the right track. Your three questions: ## 1. Inline Count Badges -- Use Plain Visible Text **Do NOT use the `aria-label` + `aria-hidden` pattern.** Use the simple version: ```html Inbox (3) ``` Here's why: The `aria-label` approach (`aria-label="Inbox, 3 items"`) has two problems: - `aria-label` **completely overrides** the visible text for screen readers. If you change the visible text but forget to update the `aria-label`, they diverge silently. This is a maintenance trap. - WCAG 2.5.3 (Label in Name) requires that the accessible name contains the visible text. With `aria-label="Inbox, 3 items"` and `aria-hidden="true"` on the count, you're fine technically -- but it's unnecessarily complex. The simple version -- `Inbox (3)` as visible text -- gives perfect screen reader output: "Inbox (3), link." That's clear, accurate, and matches what sighted users see. The `` wrapper gives you full CSS control over the visual treatment (smaller font size, muted color, different weight) without affecting the accessible name at all. This is the pattern I gave Lea as well, so you'll be aligned on the markup. ## 2. Two-Tier Visual Hierarchy -- Fine, With One Caveat Visually de-emphasizing less-used links with lighter weight or color is totally fine from an a11y perspective. Screen reader users don't experience visual weight, so this is a pure sighted-user optimization. Tab order treats all links equally. No concern there. **The one caveat**: make sure the de-emphasized links still meet WCAG AA contrast requirements. If you use a lighter color for "Waiting For" and "Someday/Maybe" link text, it still needs 4.5:1 contrast against the background. Our current `--color-primary` was already flagged and fixed for contrast (we adjusted lightness from 0.55 to 0.52 in oklch). Don't let the de-emphasized links drift below that threshold. A safer approach for de-emphasis: use **font-weight** (regular vs. semibold) or **font-size** (slightly smaller) rather than color lightness. Weight and size differences don't affect contrast ratios, so they're "free" from an a11y perspective. A primary link at `font-weight: 600` and a secondary link at `font-weight: 400` gives clear visual hierarchy without touching color at all. ## 3. Processing Flow -- IMPORTANT CORRECTION You described the inbox processing as "one question at a time with decision buttons, each choice triggers an HTMX swap to reveal the next question." This is the wizard pattern that Steve Krug and I just unified against. I want to make sure you have the updated direction: **The clarify flow is NOT one-question-at-a-time with HTMX swaps between steps.** It's a **single form with progressive disclosure via CSS `:has()`**. The radio buttons control which fields are visible. No HTMX swaps between questions. No round-trips. One form, one submit. This means your question about aria-live vs. focus management for inter-step swaps doesn't apply -- there are no inter-step swaps. The CSS shows/hides field sections instantly based on which radio button is selected. No announcement needed because the user initiated the change (they clicked a radio button) and the result is immediate and adjacent. The only HTMX swap in the clarify flow is when the user submits the form and the server returns the NEXT inbox item's clarify page. At that point, focus moves to the `

` of the new page AND the aria-live region announces the outcome (e.g., "Moved to Next Actions. Processing item 4 of 12."). Steve Krug, Carson, Lea, and I have all aligned on this pattern. Marty has the unified proposal. Just wanted to make sure you're designing for the right interaction model. For your visual design of the clarify form, think of it as: - A page header ("Process inbox item" + "Processing item 3 of 12" + "Back to inbox" link) - The captured item's text displayed prominently - A form with radio button groups that reveal/hide conditional field sections - One submit button at the bottom No step indicators, no progress bars, no multi-panel layouts. Just a form.

content.text: Heydon's response is excellent and includes an important correction on the clarify flow. Let me respond and update my design direction accordingly.

input.content:
Heydon, all three answers are clear and helpful. Let me confirm my updated design direction for each. ## 1. Inline Count Badges -- Plain Text, Agreed Simple version adopted: ```html Inbox (3) ``` The `` gives me the CSS hook I need (smaller font, muted color) without any ARIA complexity. "Inbox (3), link" is exactly what sighted users see and what screen readers announce. No divergence risk, no maintenance trap. Done. ## 2. Two-Tier Hierarchy -- Weight Over Color Your caveat is well-taken. I'll use **font-weight** as the primary de-emphasis tool instead of color: - Tier 1 (Inbox, Next Actions, Projects): `--font-semibold` (600) - Tier 2 (Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Review): `--font-normal` (400) - All links use the same color (`--color-fg-muted` at rest, `--color-fg` when active) This gives clear visual hierarchy with zero contrast risk. Weight differences are immediately perceptible to sighted users but don't change the contrast ratio at all. Better solution than what I originally proposed. ## 3. Clarify Flow -- Major Design Correction Accepted Thank you for catching this. My card-based one-question-at-a-time design was wrong -- it assumed HTMX swaps between decision steps, which is the wizard pattern you and Steve Krug rejected. **Updated design understanding:** The clarify flow is a **single form with progressive disclosure via CSS `:has()`**. Here's how I'm now thinking about the visual design: **Page structure:** ``` Process inbox item 4 of 12 remaining Back to inbox +------------------------------------------+ | "Call dentist about appointment" | | Captured 2 hours ago | +------------------------------------------+ Is this actionable? ( ) Yes ( ) No [If "Yes" selected -- revealed via :has()] What type? ( ) Next action ( ) Project ( ) Delegate [If "Next action" -- revealed via nested :has()] Title: [___________________________] Context: @computer @home @errands @phone @anywhere [If "No" selected -- revealed via :has()] ( ) Trash ( ) Someday/Maybe [ Clarify ] Skip ``` **Key design differences from my earlier proposal:** - The decision buttons become **radio buttons**, not large action buttons. Radio buttons are the right semantic element for "pick one option." - Conditional fields **appear instantly** below the selected radio, no network round-trip, no loading state. - One **"Clarify" submit button** at the bottom of the form (not per-step action buttons). - "Skip" as a secondary text link, not a button. - The captured item stays visible at the top throughout -- it's the context for all decisions below. **Visual treatment of radio groups:** - Radio buttons with clear, visible labels. No custom styled radio buttons that break a11y -- use native radios with CSS that enhances their appearance. - Each radio group has a visible fieldset legend ("Is this actionable?", "What type?"). - Conditionally revealed sections have a slight left indent or subtle left border to show they're dependent on the parent choice. **The only HTMX interaction:** Form submit -> server processes -> returns the NEXT inbox item's clarify page. Focus moves to `

`, aria-live announces "Moved to Next Actions. Processing item 5 of 12." This is simpler, more accessible, and works without JS out of the box. The CSS `:has()` is progressive enhancement -- without it, all fields are visible (which still works, just noisier). One question: for the `:has()` progressive enhancement, should the no-JS fallback show all fields visible with a note like "Select an option above to see relevant fields"? Or should we just show everything and let users fill in what's relevant?
input.summary: Accepted all three answers, corrected clarify flow design

Focus moves to `

The CSS `:has()` is progressive enhancement -- without it, all fields are visible (which still works, just noisier). content.text:
Heydon's feedback led to three important design updates:

1. **Count badges**: Plain text `Inbox (3)` with a `<span class="nav-count">` for CSS styling. No `aria-label` / `aria-hidden` complexity.

2. **Nav hierarchy via weight, not color**: Tier 1 links at `--font-semibold` (600), Tier 2 at `--font-normal` (400), all same color. Zero contrast risk.

3. **Clarify flow completely redesigned**: Dropped the card-based one-question-at-a-time wizard. Now a single form with radio buttons and CSS `:has()` progressive disclosure. No HTMX swaps between steps. One submit button. Native radios with fieldset legends. The no-JS fallback shows all fields visible. Asked Heydon a follow-up about the best fallback UX.

This was the biggest design correction this session -- the clarify flow went from a multi-step HTMX wizard to a single-page form. The result is simpler, more accessible, works without JS, and has no focus management or aria-live concerns between steps. Only the form submission (which loads the next inbox item) needs HTMX + announcements.
